pattern: XOR-ing a value across every triplet combination collapses to something much smaller once you track a single bit's parity instead of simulating all n^3 triplets, and that parity argument reduces all the way down to one linear XOR pass over the array.
hints: Instead of simulating every triplet, pick one bit position and ask: across all n^3 triplets, does that bit end up set an odd or an even number of times? Only the parity matters for XOR.
For a fixed bit, count how many array values have that bit set. Work out, in terms of that count and n, how many of the n^3 triplets make the bit 1 -- then reduce that count modulo 2.
The parity you land on only depends on whether the count of set-bit values is itself odd or even -- which is exactly what XOR-ing every array element together already tells you, one bit at a time.

edge_inputs.description: single element at the maximum allowed value
several elements all at the minimum allowed value
a pair of equal values whose plain XOR cancels to zero
distinct powers of two with no overlapping bits
an odd count of repeated values mixed with a lone different value

stress.description: random array spanning the full length and value range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
